_description: Actual match outcomes and per-question RBP results from the JTC SportsPredict platform

question_results.text: Will Harry Kane (England) score a goal?
question_results.note: Kept close to market rather than bumping despite his 75% group-stage scoring rate; crowd's higher number was better calibrated for this specific open, high-scoring match.

question_results.text: Will Jude Bellingham (England) have 2 or more shots on target?
Will England advance to the quarterfinals?
Will Raúl Jiménez (Mexico) score a goal?
question_results.note: Both strikers (Kane AND Jiménez) scored -- underpriced the match's overall attacking/scoring environment.

question_results.text: Will Julián Quiñones (Mexico) have 1 or more shots on target?
question_results.note: Personal-floor logic (4/4 games hit) validated -- biggest win of the match.

question_results.text: Will the match have 2 or fewer total goals?
question_results.note: Match finished 2-3 (5 total goals) -- badly underestimated the scoring environment, more confidently wrong than the crowd.

question_results.text: Will the match be tied at halftime?
Will there be 4 or more total cards shown?
Will England have more corner kicks than Mexico?
Will a goal be scored after the second hydration break?
Will England have 5 or more shots on target?
question_results.note: BIGGEST LOSS OF THE MATCH. Deliberately stayed at market (0.40) per Cluster-B discipline instead of blending toward England's own empirical SOT average (0.80-0.90) -- this time the discipline cost us, the mirror image of the SAME decision winning big in the BRA-NOR match the same day (Brazil 6+ SOT, +22.57). See chat analysis for the nuance this reveals.

question_results.text: Will the match be decided by a penalty shootout?
Will there be more total cards than total goals?
Will there be 18 or more total shots?

key_lessons: 8/15 beat crowd, a much more modest showing than BRA-NOR the same day. Net positive only because of two big wins (Quiñones +19.42, 18+ shots +15.82) offsetting one big loss (England 5+ SOT -16.79).

SINGLE ROOT CAUSE explains 4 of the 5 losses: Q1 (Kane scores), Q4 (Jimenez scores), Q6 (2-or-fewer goals), and Q11 (England 5+ SOT) are all different symptoms of the same underlying miscalibration -- underestimating how open/high-scoring this specific match would be (final: 2-3, 5 goals, both strikers scoring, England well past the SOT threshold). This was one systemic miscalibration wearing four different masks, not four independent mistakes.

The England 5+ SOT loss (-16.79) is the direct mirror-image test of the SAME Cluster-B discipline decision that won +22.57 on Brazil 6+ SOT the same day. Genuinely mixed evidence within a single day -- worth treating this rule as directional guidance, not an absolute law, and reading match-specific goal/attack-environment signals more carefully going forward.
